## Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Side-by-Side Comparison
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/chapter-7-vs-chapter-13.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City

The two most common types of consumer bankruptcy work very differently. This side-by-side comparison covers eligibility, duration, cost, property, discharge scope, and success rates for Kansas City filers on both sides of the state line.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

~95%

Ch. 7 Discharge Rate

40.4%

Ch. 13 Dismissal (W.D. Mo.)

Side-by-Side Comparison Table

FactorChapter 7Chapter 13

What it doesEliminates most unsecured debt through liquidationReorganizes debt into a 3-5 year repayment plan

Duration3-4 months filing to discharge3-5 years to complete plan and receive discharge

Means test required?Yes - must be below median income or pass expense calculationNo means test, but income determines plan length and payment amount

Property at risk?Non-exempt property may be sold by trusteeKeep all property as long as plan payments are made

Filing fee$338$313 (can be paid through plan)

Attorney fees (KC range)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,000

Debts dischargedCredit cards, medical bills, personal loans, old utility billsSame unsecured debts, plus can catch up on secured debt arrears

Mortgage arrearsCannot catch up - foreclosure may proceed after stay liftsCan cure arrears over plan and keep home

Car loan defaultMust reaffirm, redeem, or surrenderCan cure default and keep vehicle through plan

Tax debtOnly taxes meeting the 3-year/2-year/240-day rulesCan pay priority taxes in full over 3-5 years without interest

Co-signer protectionNone - creditors can pursue co-signers immediatelyCo-debtor stay protects co-signers during plan

Second mortgage strippingNot availableCan strip off wholly unsecured junior liens

Repeat filing eligibility8 years after prior Ch. 7 discharge; 6 years after Ch. 132 years after prior Ch. 13 discharge; 4 years after Ch. 7

W.D. Mo. dismissal rate~5%40.4%

D. Kan. dismissal rate~4%31.4%

Credit report impactStays on report for 10 years from filingStays on report for 7 years from filing

When Chapter 7 Is the Better Choice

Your income is below the Missouri or Kansas median for your household size, so you pass the means test automatically

Most of your debt is unsecured - credit cards, medical bills, personal loans

You do not have significant non-exempt assets that a trustee would liquidate

You want a fresh start quickly - Chapter 7 discharges in about 3-4 months

You are not behind on mortgage or car payments and can continue making current payments

You do not have co-signers you need to protect from creditor collection

Full Chapter 7 guide for Kansas City →

When Chapter 13 Is the Better Choice

You are behind on your mortgage and facing foreclosure - Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears over 3-5 years while keeping your home

You are behind on car payments and facing repossession - Chapter 13 can cure the default

Your income is above the means test threshold and you do not qualify for Chapter 7

You have non-exempt property you want to keep (equity above exemption limits)

You owe priority taxes that cannot be discharged - Chapter 13 lets you pay them over time

You have a co-signer on consumer debts and want the co-debtor stay to protect them

You have a wholly unsecured second mortgage you want to strip off

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the last 8 years and cannot file Chapter 7 again yet

Full Chapter 13 guide for Kansas City →

Kansas City Dismissal Rates: A Critical Difference

The success rate for each chapter varies dramatically, and Kansas City filers should understand these numbers before choosing.

Chapter 7 Success Rate

Approximately 95% of Chapter 7 cases in both W.D. Mo. and D. Kan. result in a discharge. Most cases that do not result in discharge are voluntarily dismissed by the debtor, not denied by the court.

Chapter 7 cases are resolved quickly. There is no multi-year plan to fail. Once you file and attend the 341 meeting, discharge is nearly automatic unless there is a specific objection.

Chapter 13 Completion Rate

Chapter 13 has a much lower success rate. In the Western District of Missouri, 40.4% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before the debtor receives a discharge. The District of Kansas is somewhat better at 31.4%.

Common reasons for Chapter 13 failure include job loss, income changes, plan payments that were too high to sustain, or simply running out of financial stamina over 3-5 years.

These numbers are based on FJC Integrated Database data covering 2008-2024. The quality of your legal representation matters significantly for Chapter 13 outcomes.

Eligibility Requirements

Chapter 7 Eligibility

Must pass the means test (income below state median, or pass the expense calculation)

Must complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing

Cannot have received a Chapter 7 discharge within the last 8 years

Cannot have received a Chapter 13 discharge within the last 6 years

Cannot have had a case dismissed for abuse within the last 180 days

2026 Missouri and Kansas income limits →

Chapter 13 Eligibility

No means test required

Must have regular income sufficient to fund a plan

Secured debts must be under $2,750,000 and unsecured debts under $2,750,000 (2026 limits)

Must complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing

Cannot have received a Chapter 13 discharge within the last 2 years

Cannot have received a Chapter 7 discharge within the last 4 years

Must be current on tax filing obligations

Cost Comparison for Kansas City Filers

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (KC typical)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,000

Credit counseling (2 courses)$25 - $50$25 - $50

Fee waiver available?Yes, if below 150% of poverty guidelinesNo waiver, but filing fee can be paid through plan

Payment timingAttorney fees due before filing in most casesAttorney fees can be paid through plan over time

Total estimated cost$1,363 - $2,388$2,838 - $4,363

Full cost breakdown for Kansas City →

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the main difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 reorganizes your debts into a 3-5 year repayment plan that lets you keep your property. Chapter 7 requires passing a means test; Chapter 13 does not.

Which chapter has a higher success rate in Kansas City?

Chapter 7 has a significantly higher success rate. Approximately 95% of Chapter 7 cases result in discharge. Chapter 13 has a 40.4% dismissal rate in W.D. Mo. and 31.4% in D. Kan. The quality of legal representation matters significantly for Chapter 13 outcomes.

Can I keep my house and car in Chapter 7?

You can keep your house and car if your equity is within exemption limits. Missouri allows $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle exemptions. Kansas has an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000 vehicle exemption. If you are behind on payments, Chapter 13 may be better because it lets you catch up over time.

How much does each chapter cost in Kansas City?

Chapter 7 has a $338 filing fee and attorney fees of $1,000-$2,000. Chapter 13 has a $313 filing fee and attorney fees of $2,500-$4,000. Both require two credit counseling courses ($25-$50 total). Full cost breakdown.

What debts can Chapter 13 handle that Chapter 7 cannot?

Chapter 13 can catch up on mortgage arrears to prevent foreclosure, cure car loan defaults, pay priority tax debts over time, and protect co-signers from collection. Chapter 13 can also strip off wholly unsecured junior liens. Learn what debts cannot be discharged in either chapter.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility, or explore the means test to see if you qualify for Chapter 7.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Kansas City residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Kansas City, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Missouri or Kansas exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility. If you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan, you may be required to file Chapter 13 instead

The median income figures are updated periodically. For a single filer in Missouri, the current median is approximately $55,000/year. For Kansas, it is approximately $57,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Missouri Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. Missouri uses its own exemption system (you cannot use federal exemptions).

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000 equity

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Clothing$1,500

Wildcard$600 + up to $1,250 unused homestead

Tools of trade$3,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Public benefitsFully exempt

Kansas exemptions are significantly more generous, particularly the unlimited homestead exemption. Full exemption comparison.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity, garnishments, lawsuits, and foreclosure proceedings.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes. Held at the courthouse or by phone/video.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts. Case closed shortly after.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your household income to the Missouri or Kansas median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Kansas City?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Missouri?

Missouri exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $15,000, one vehicle up to $3,000, household goods up to $3,000, clothing up to $1,500, a wildcard exemption of $600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead, tools of trade up to $3,000, and certain retirement accounts. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist: Kansas City - Documents and Steps
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/checklist.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy Filing Checklist: Kansas City - Documents and Steps | Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide›Bankruptcy Filing Checklist: Kansas City

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist: Kansas City

Everything you need to gather, do, and know - before, during, and after your bankruptcy case. Print this page for a paper checklist you can mark up.

PHASE 1: BEFORE FILING

Documents to Gather

Federal tax returns - last 2 yearsBoth years are required. If you have not filed, file them first.

State tax returns - last 2 yearsMissouri or Kansas, depending on which side you live on.

Pay stubs - last 6 monthsFrom all employers. If self-employed, profit and loss statements.

Bank statements - last 6 monthsAll accounts: checking, savings, investment, business. Every account at every bank.

Vehicle titles and registrationFor every vehicle you own. Include VIN, year, make, model, mileage, and estimated value.

Mortgage statement or lease agreementMost recent statement showing balance, monthly payment, interest rate, and arrears (if any).

All credit card statementsCurrent statements for every card, even if the balance is zero. Note the creditor name, account number, and balance.

All loan statementsCar loans, personal loans, student loans, 401(k) loans, family loans. Include creditor name, balance, monthly payment.

Medical billsAll outstanding medical debts. Include provider name, original amount, collection agency (if applicable), and current balance.

Court judgments and garnishmentsAny judgments against you, including case numbers. Current garnishment orders with employer.

Property valuationsHome value (Zillow estimate or recent appraisal). Vehicle values (NADA or KBB). Estimated value of household goods, electronics, jewelry, tools.

Insurance policiesLife insurance (whole life policies have cash value), health, auto, homeowner's/renter's.

Retirement account statements401(k), IRA, pension. These are generally protected in bankruptcy but must be listed.

Divorce decree or separation agreementIf applicable. Includes support obligations and property division.

Child support ordersCurrent orders and any arrears.

Prior bankruptcy filingsCase numbers, dates, districts, and outcomes of any prior bankruptcy cases. This affects your eligibility under 1328(f).

Steps Before Filing

Complete credit counseling courseMust be from a DOJ-approved agency. Takes 1-2 hours online. Cost: $15-25. Must be completed within 180 days before filing. Keep the certificate.

Take the means testDetermines Chapter 7 eligibility based on your income vs. the Missouri or Kansas median. Your attorney will calculate this. meanstest.org

File all required tax returnsAll federal and state returns for the past 4 years must be filed. The trustee will check.

Stop using credit cardsCharges made shortly before filing can be challenged as fraudulent. Stop all credit use immediately.

Do not pay back family membersPayments to insiders within 1 year can be recovered by the trustee. Do not repay family or friends.

Do not transfer propertyDo not sell, give away, or transfer any property. The trustee can reverse transfers made within 2 years (or longer for fraud).

Do not take on new debtNew debt incurred when you know you are filing can be challenged as non-dischargeable.

Get property valuationsCheck NADA for vehicles, Zillow/Redfin for home value. These go on Schedule A/B.

List all creditorsEvery person and company you owe money to, including disputed debts. If a creditor is not listed, the debt may not be discharged.

PHASE 2: DURING YOUR CASE

After Filing Through Discharge

Attend the 341 meeting of creditorsAbout 30 days after filing. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Full 341 meeting guide

Complete debtor education courseSecond required course (different from pre-filing credit counseling). Takes about 2 hours. Must be done before discharge. Cost: $10-25.

Respond to trustee document requestsThe trustee may request additional documents (bank statements, tax returns, appraisals). Respond promptly - delays can cause problems.

Continue paying current obligationsKeep paying mortgage, car payment, utilities, insurance, and child support. The automatic stay does not excuse you from current obligations.

Make Chapter 13 plan payments on timeChapter 13 only. First payment due within 30 days of filing. Do not miss payments - it can result in case dismissal.

Maintain insurance on secured propertyKeep insurance current on your home and car. Losing insurance can be grounds for the lender to seek relief from the automatic stay.

Do not incur new debt without court permissionChapter 13 filers cannot take on new debt (loans, credit cards) without trustee/court approval.

File amended schedules if anything changesNew job, inheritance, tax refund, change of address - report changes to your attorney for amendment.

File all tax returns on timeChapter 13 filers must file all tax returns on time during the plan. Failure to file can result in dismissal.

PHASE 3: AFTER DISCHARGE

Rebuilding and Monitoring

Save your discharge order permanentlyKeep both a physical copy and a digital copy. You may need it years from now if a creditor tries to collect on a discharged debt.

Check credit reports (60-90 days after discharge)Pull all three reports (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) at annualcreditreport.com. Discharged debts should show $0 balance with a notation of "included in bankruptcy."

Dispute credit report errorsIf any discharged debt still shows a balance or "past due" status, file a dispute with the credit bureau. Include a copy of your discharge order.

Begin rebuilding creditConsider a secured credit card (you deposit money as collateral). Make small purchases and pay the full balance each month. Credit improvement typically begins within 12-18 months.

Create a monthly budgetNow that your debts are eliminated or restructured, build a sustainable budget. Track income and expenses to avoid falling back into debt.

Build an emergency fundStart small - even $500-$1,000 provides a buffer against unexpected expenses that could otherwise force new debt.

Monitor for discharge violationsIf any creditor contacts you about a discharged debt, they are violating the discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524). Document the contact and consult an attorney - you may be entitled to damages.

Know your eligibility for future filingA Chapter 7 discharge bars another Chapter 7 for 8 years and another Chapter 13 for 6 years. A Chapter 13 discharge bars another Chapter 13 for 2 years and another Chapter 7 for 6 years. Check at 1328f.com.

Things to avoid before filing:

Do not pay back family or friends (preferential transfer - trustee can claw it back)

Do not transfer property to anyone (fraudulent transfer - trustee can reverse it)

Do not use credit cards (recent charges may be non-dischargeable)

Do not take cash advances (presumed fraudulent within 70 days)

Do not make luxury purchases (presumed non-dischargeable within 90 days)

Do not hide assets (federal crime under 18 U.S.C. section 152)

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Kansas City?

2 years of tax returns, 6 months of pay stubs, 6 months of bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage/lease statements, all credit card and loan statements, medical bills, court judgments, property valuations, and insurance policies. See the full checklist above.

What steps do I need to complete before filing?

Complete a credit counseling course, gather all documents, take the means test, file all required tax returns, get property valuations, and stop using credit cards and making preferential payments.

What happens during a bankruptcy case?

Attend the 341 meeting, complete the debtor education course, respond to trustee requests, continue making current payments, and (in Chapter 13) make plan payments on time.

What should I do after bankruptcy discharge?

Save your discharge order, check credit reports after 60-90 days, dispute errors, begin rebuilding credit with a secured card, create a budget, build an emergency fund, and monitor for discharge violations.

Do I need to file my tax returns before filing bankruptcy?

Yes. All required federal and state returns for the last 4 years must be filed. The trustee will request copies. Unfiled returns can result in case dismissal.

Can I use credit cards before filing?

Stop using credit cards as soon as you decide to file. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent.

Can I pay back family members before filing?

No. Payments to insiders within one year can be recovered by the trustee as preferential transfers. Do not selectively repay anyone before filing.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your income to the Missouri or Kansas median for your household size. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7. If above, you may still qualify after expense deductions. meanstest.org.

Print this checklist: Use your browser's print function (Ctrl+P or Cmd+P) to print this page. The print layout removes the navigation and formatting for a clean checklist you can write on.

## Bankruptcy in Baldwin City, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/baldwin-city.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Baldwin City, KS

Bankruptcy in Baldwin City, KS

Baldwin City is a small community in Douglas County, Kansas, home to Baker University - the oldest university in Kansas. Located south of Lawrence along US-56, it has a college-town character with affordable housing.

Key Filing Information

County

Douglas County

Population: 4,700 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66006

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Baldwin City

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit (reasonable necessity)

WildcardNone (not needed with unlimited homestead)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions. Kansas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Baldwin City, KS can protect unlimited home equity and up to $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. Someone across the state line in Missouri can protect only $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Same metro area, dramatically different bankruptcy outcomes.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Baldwin City

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - lower than the Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - significantly higher dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Douglas County Courthouse - 1100 Massachusetts St, Lawrence, KS 66044

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave, Topeka, KS 66603 (serves statewide)

Kansas 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Baldwin City, KS cases?

Baldwin City is in Douglas County, which files in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What exemptions apply in Baldwin City, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply. Unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban), $20,000 vehicle ($40,000 married), fully exempt retirement. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Baldwin City compare to Missouri suburbs?

Kansas residents benefit from an unlimited homestead exemption, while Missouri suburbs are limited to $15,000. Kansas vehicle exemption is $20,000/$40,000 versus Missouri s $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Missouri instead of Kansas?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Missouri, you must reside there at least 730 days before Missouri exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Baldwin City specifically?

Baldwin City is a small college town with affordable housing. Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption means even modest homes are fully protected. Student loans from Baker University are generally not dischargeable, but other debts can be eliminated.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Basehor, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/basehor.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Basehor, KS

Bankruptcy in Basehor, KS

Basehor is a small city in Leavenworth County, Kansas, between Kansas City and Leavenworth along K-32 and K-7 highways. It has grown rapidly as a bedroom community with new residential developments and a strong school district.

Key Filing Information

County

Leavenworth County

Population: 6,700 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66007

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Basehor

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit (reasonable necessity)

WildcardNone (not needed with unlimited homestead)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions. Kansas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Basehor, KS can protect unlimited home equity and up to $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. Someone across the state line in Missouri can protect only $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Same metro area, dramatically different bankruptcy outcomes.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Basehor

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - significantly higher dismissal rate

Kansas 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Basehor, KS cases?

Basehor is in Leavenworth County, which files in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What exemptions apply in Basehor, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply. Unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban), $20,000 vehicle ($40,000 married), fully exempt retirement. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Basehor compare to Missouri suburbs?

Kansas residents benefit from an unlimited homestead exemption, while Missouri suburbs are limited to $15,000. Kansas vehicle exemption is $20,000/$40,000 versus Missouri s $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Missouri instead of Kansas?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Missouri, you must reside there at least 730 days before Missouri exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Basehor specifically?

Basehor is a growing Leavenworth County suburb. Like all Kansas residents, Basehor filers benefit from the unlimited homestead exemption and generous vehicle exemption. Cases are filed in the District of Kansas.

## Bankruptcy in Belton, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/belton.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Belton, MO

Bankruptcy in Belton, MO

Belton is a city in Cass County on the southern edge of the Kansas City metro area, with a population of about 23,500. It combines small-town character with suburban convenience.

Key Filing Information

County

Cass County

Population: 23,480 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64012

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Belton

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Belton, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity (up to 1 acre urban). Same metro area. Same housing market. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Belton

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Belton City Hall - 506 Main St, Belton, MO 64012

Cass County Courthouse - 2501 W Wall St, Harrisonville, MO 64701

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Belton, MO cases?

Belton is in Cass County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. The W.D. Mo. has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Belton, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Belton compare to Kansas suburbs for bankruptcy?

Belton residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs across the state line have an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file in the district where you live. Belton residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose Kansas to get better exemptions. If you move to Kansas, you must live there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Belton specifically?

Belton is in Cass County but still files through the Western District of Missouri. Cass County residents have the same Missouri exemptions and file at the same Kansas City courthouse.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Blue Springs, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/blue-springs.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Blue Springs, MO

Bankruptcy in Blue Springs, MO

Blue Springs is a suburb of Kansas City located in eastern Jackson County with a population of about 57,000. It is a family-oriented community with a mix of residential developments and local businesses.

Key Filing Information

County

Jackson County

Population: 57,006 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64013, 64014, 64015

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Blue Springs

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Blue Springs, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity (up to 1 acre urban). Same metro area. Same housing market. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Blue Springs

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Blue Springs Municipal Court - 903 W Main St, Blue Springs, MO 64015

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Blue Springs, MO cases?

Blue Springs is in Jackson County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. The W.D. Mo. has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Blue Springs, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Blue Springs compare to Kansas suburbs for bankruptcy?

Blue Springs residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs across the state line have an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file in the district where you live. Blue Springs residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose Kansas to get better exemptions. If you move to Kansas, you must live there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Blue Springs specifically?

Blue Springs residents face the same Missouri exemption limits as all Jackson County filers. The $3,000 vehicle exemption is particularly relevant since many residents commute to Kansas City for work.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Bonner Springs, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/bonner-springs.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Bonner Springs, KS

Bankruptcy in Bonner Springs, KS

Bonner Springs straddles Wyandotte and Leavenworth Counties, with a population of about 7,700. Located west of Kansas City, KS, it is home to the National Agricultural Center and Providence Medical Center Amphitheater.

Key Filing Information

County

Wyandotte County / Leavenworth County

Population: 7,738 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City, KS Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66012

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Bonner Springs

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUNLIMITED (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit on necessary items

Personal Property$20,000 total (single) / $40,000 (married)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions.

The Kansas Advantage

Bonner Springs residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption. Across the state line in Missouri, homeowners can protect only $15,000 in home equity. This is one of the most dramatic exemption differences in the country.

Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married). Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Bonner Springs

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower than Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - higher dismissal rate across the state line

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Bonner Springs City Hall - 205 E 2nd St, Bonner Springs, KS 66012

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave #200, Topeka, KS 66603 (statewide intake)

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Bonner Springs, KS cases?

Bonner Springs is in Wyandotte County / Leavenworth County, which files in the District of Kansas. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. The D. Kan. has a 31.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Bonner Springs, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply: unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural), $20,000 vehicle (single) or $40,000 (married), fully exempt retirement accounts. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does the Kansas side compare to Missouri for bankruptcy?

Bonner Springs residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption versus Missouri's $15,000, and Kansas's $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. The D. Kan. also has a lower Chapter 13 dismissal rate (31.4% vs 40.4%). Full comparison.

Can I use Missouri exemptions if I recently moved from Missouri?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Kansas for at least 730 days (2 years) before filing, you may need to use the exemptions from your prior state. If you moved from Missouri to Kansas recently, you may still be subject to Missouri's lower exemptions. Consult an attorney about your specific timeline.

What about Bonner Springs specifically?

Bonner Springs residents, whether in the Wyandotte or Leavenworth County portion, all file in the District of Kansas. Kansas exemptions apply regardless of which county within Kansas you live in.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Buckner, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/buckner.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Buckner, MO

Bankruptcy in Buckner, MO

Buckner is a small rural-suburban community in eastern Jackson County, located along Highway 24 east of Independence. It offers a quiet, small-town lifestyle within commuting distance of Kansas City.

Key Filing Information

County

Jackson County

Population: 4,200 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64016

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Buckner

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Buckner, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas: $20,000 / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Buckner

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Jackson County Courthouse (Eastern) - 308 W Kansas Ave, Independence, MO 64050

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Missouri 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Buckner, MO cases?

Buckner is in Jackson County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Buckner, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Buckner compare to Kansas suburbs?

Buckner residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Buckner specifically?

Buckner is a small, affordable community east of Independence. Most homeowners here have equity well within Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption, making Chapter 7 accessible. The nearest federal courthouse is in downtown Kansas City.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in De Soto, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/de-soto.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » De Soto, KS

Bankruptcy in De Soto, KS

De Soto is a city in western Johnson County, Kansas, along the Kansas River. It has been in the national spotlight as the site of the massive Panasonic EV battery plant, which is expected to transform the local economy.

Key Filing Information

County

Johnson County

Population: 6,600 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66018

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in De Soto

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit (reasonable necessity)

WildcardNone (not needed with unlimited homestead)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions. Kansas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation.

The State Line Difference

Someone in De Soto, KS can protect unlimited home equity and up to $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. Someone across the state line in Missouri can protect only $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Same metro area, dramatically different bankruptcy outcomes.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in De Soto

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - lower than the Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - significantly higher dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Johnson County Courthouse - 100 N Kansas Ave, Olathe, KS 66061

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave, Topeka, KS 66603 (serves statewide)

Kansas 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles De Soto, KS cases?

De Soto is in Johnson County, which files in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What exemptions apply in De Soto, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply. Unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban), $20,000 vehicle ($40,000 married), fully exempt retirement. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does De Soto compare to Missouri suburbs?

Kansas residents benefit from an unlimited homestead exemption, while Missouri suburbs are limited to $15,000. Kansas vehicle exemption is $20,000/$40,000 versus Missouri s $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Missouri instead of Kansas?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Missouri, you must reside there at least 730 days before Missouri exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about De Soto specifically?

De Soto is experiencing major economic growth from the Panasonic plant. Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption protects residents' homes even as property values rise. The $20,000 vehicle exemption also far exceeds Missouri's $3,000.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Bankruptcy in Edgerton, KS

Edgerton is a small city in southwest Johnson County, Kansas, that has gained national attention as the site of the BNSF Logistics Park - one of the largest intermodal facilities in North America. The community is transitioning from rural to industrial-suburban.

## Bankruptcy in Excelsior Springs, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/excelsior-springs.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Excelsior Springs, MO

Bankruptcy in Excelsior Springs, MO

Excelsior Springs is a small city straddling Clay and Ray Counties, northeast of Kansas City, with a population of about 11,600. It is known for its historic mineral springs and resort heritage.

Key Filing Information

County

Clay County / Ray County

Population: 11,641 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64024

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Excelsior Springs

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Excelsior Springs, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity (up to 1 acre urban). Same metro area. Same housing market. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Excelsior Springs

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Excelsior Springs City Hall - 201 E Broadway, Excelsior Springs, MO 64024

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Excelsior Springs, MO cases?

Excelsior Springs is in Clay County / Ray County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. The W.D. Mo. has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Excelsior Springs, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Excelsior Springs compare to Kansas suburbs for bankruptcy?

Excelsior Springs residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs across the state line have an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file in the district where you live. Excelsior Springs residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose Kansas to get better exemptions. If you move to Kansas, you must live there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Excelsior Springs specifically?

Excelsior Springs has a lower cost of living than many KC suburbs, and many properties may fall within Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Residents in the Ray County portion still file through the Western District of Missouri.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Gardner, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/gardner.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Gardner, KS

Bankruptcy in Gardner, KS

Gardner is a growing suburb in southwestern Johnson County with a population of about 24,800. It has experienced rapid residential growth and is one of the fastest-growing cities in the KC metro.

Key Filing Information

County

Johnson County

Population: 24,767 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City, KS Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66030

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Gardner

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUNLIMITED (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit on necessary items

Personal Property$20,000 total (single) / $40,000 (married)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions.

The Kansas Advantage

Gardner residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption. Across the state line in Missouri, homeowners can protect only $15,000 in home equity. This is one of the most dramatic exemption differences in the country.

Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married). Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Gardner

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower than Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - higher dismissal rate across the state line

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Gardner City Hall - 120 E Main St, Gardner, KS 66030

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave #200, Topeka, KS 66603 (statewide intake)

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Gardner, KS cases?

Gardner is in Johnson County, which files in the District of Kansas. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. The D. Kan. has a 31.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Gardner, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply: unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural), $20,000 vehicle (single) or $40,000 (married), fully exempt retirement accounts. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does the Kansas side compare to Missouri for bankruptcy?

Gardner residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption versus Missouri's $15,000, and Kansas's $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. The D. Kan. also has a lower Chapter 13 dismissal rate (31.4% vs 40.4%). Full comparison.

Can I use Missouri exemptions if I recently moved from Missouri?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Kansas for at least 730 days (2 years) before filing, you may need to use the exemptions from your prior state. If you moved from Missouri to Kansas recently, you may still be subject to Missouri's lower exemptions. Consult an attorney about your specific timeline.

What about Gardner specifically?

Gardner's rapid growth means many residents have recently purchased homes. Even with newer mortgages, Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption provides strong protection that Missouri cannot match.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Gladstone, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/gladstone.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Gladstone, MO

Bankruptcy in Gladstone, MO

Gladstone is a northern suburb of Kansas City in Clay County with a population of about 27,500. It is primarily residential and known for its proximity to the Kansas City metro's commercial centers.

Key Filing Information

County

Clay County

Population: 27,560 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64118, 64119

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Gladstone

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Gladstone, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity (up to 1 acre urban). Same metro area. Same housing market. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Gladstone

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Gladstone City Hall - 7010 N Holmes St, Gladstone, MO 64118

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Gladstone, MO cases?

Gladstone is in Clay County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. The W.D. Mo. has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Gladstone, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Gladstone compare to Kansas suburbs for bankruptcy?

Gladstone residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs across the state line have an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file in the district where you live. Gladstone residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose Kansas to get better exemptions. If you move to Kansas, you must live there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Gladstone specifically?

Gladstone's housing market offers relatively affordable homes compared to other KC suburbs, but Missouri's $15,000 homestead cap still limits protection for most homeowners.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

Bankruptcy in Grain Valley, MO

Grain Valley is a fast-growing eastern suburb in Jackson County, located along I-70 east of Blue Springs. Known for its family-friendly atmosphere and award-winning school district, it has become one of the fastest-growing communities in the KC metro with a population of about 15,300.

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Harrisonville is the county seat of Cass County, located about 35 miles south of downtown Kansas City. It is a regional center for southern Cass County with a historic courthouse square and a mix of rural and suburban character.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Harrisonville, MO cases?

Harrisonville is in Cass County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Harrisonville, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Harrisonville compare to Kansas suburbs?

Harrisonville residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Harrisonville specifically?

Harrisonville is the Cass County seat with relatively affordable housing. Many residents have home equity within Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. State court matters go to the local courthouse, but federal bankruptcy cases go to KC.

## Bankruptcy in Independence, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/independence.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Independence, MO

Bankruptcy in Independence, MO

Independence is the fifth-largest city in Missouri and the county seat of Jackson County. Located just east of Kansas City, it has a population of over 123,000 and is known as the hometown of President Harry S. Truman.

Key Filing Information

County

Jackson County

Population: 123,011 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Someone in Independence, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity (up to 1 acre urban). Same metro area. Same housing market. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Independence, MO cases?

Independence is in Jackson County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. The W.D. Mo. has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Independence, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Independence compare to Kansas suburbs for bankruptcy?

Independence residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs across the state line have an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file in the district where you live. Independence residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose Kansas to get better exemptions. If you move to Kansas, you must live there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Independence specifically?

Independence residents often carry mortgage debt on older homes. With Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption, many homeowners with even modest equity may need Chapter 13 to protect their property.

## Kansas City Area Bankruptcy Guides by City - MO &amp; KS Suburbs
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities

Kansas City Area Bankruptcy Guides by City

The Kansas City metro straddles two states with dramatically different bankruptcy laws. Find your city below for a guide specific to your location, county, courthouse, and exemptions.

The State Line Makes All the Difference

Kansas City is one of the most striking examples in the country of how a state line changes bankruptcy outcomes. Someone in Raytown, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Someone in Prairie Village, KS - 15 minutes away - can protect unlimited home equity and $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. The Missouri side also has a higher Chapter 13 dismissal rate (40.4% vs 31.4%).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Missouri Side - W.D. Mo.

These cities file in the Western District of Missouri at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle.

Independence

Jackson County - MO

$15K homestead

Lee's Summit

Jackson County - MO

$15K homestead

Blue Springs

Jackson County - MO

$15K homestead

Liberty

Clay County - MO

$15K homestead

Gladstone

Clay County - MO

$15K homestead

Raytown

Jackson County - MO

$15K homestead

Grandview

Jackson County - MO

$15K homestead

Belton

Cass County - MO

$15K homestead

Raymore

Cass County - MO

$15K homestead

Excelsior Springs

Clay County / Ray County - MO

$15K homestead

North Kansas City

Clay County - MO

$15K homestead

Parkville

Platte County - MO

$15K homestead

Smithville

Clay County - MO

These cities file in the District of Kansas at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Kansas exemptions apply: unlimited homestead, $20,000/$40,000 vehicle.

Quick Comparison

FactorMissouri SideKansas Side

Homestead Exemption$15,000Unlimited (1 acre urban)

Vehicle Exemption$3,000$20,000 / $40,000 (married)

Ch. 13 Dismissal Rate40.4%31.4%

Courthouse400 E 9th St, KC MO500 State Ave, KC KS

Federal Exemptions Allowed?NoNo

Frequently Asked Questions

Does it matter which side of the state line I live on?

Yes - dramatically. Missouri suburbs have a $15,000 homestead exemption and $3,000 vehicle exemption. Kansas suburbs have an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. You must file where you live; you cannot choose the other state's court. Full comparison.

Can I move to Kansas to get better exemptions?

You can, but timing matters. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3), you must live in the new state for at least 730 days (2 years) before its exemptions apply. Moving right before filing will not give you Kansas exemptions.

Why is the dismissal rate different on each side?

Multiple factors contribute: different local legal cultures, different trustee practices, different exemption levels affecting plan feasibility, and different attorney pools. The dismissal rate data comes from the Federal Judicial Center (2008-2024).

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in your state for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.
