Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Riverside, MO

Bankruptcy in Riverside, MO

Riverside is a small city in Platte County along the Missouri River, just northwest of downtown Kansas City. Despite its small size, it is home to the Argosy Casino and a significant commercial corridor along I-635.

Key Filing Information

County

Platte County

Population: 3,400 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64150

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Riverside

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Riverside, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas: $20,000 / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Riverside

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Platte County Courthouse - 415 Third St, Platte City, MO 64079

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Missouri 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Riverside, MO cases?

Riverside is in Platte County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Riverside, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Riverside compare to Kansas suburbs?

Riverside residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Riverside specifically?

Riverside is a small Platte County city near downtown KC. Its commercial development keeps property values relatively stable. Homeowners should check whether their equity exceeds Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption before choosing between Chapter 7 and 13.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Shawnee, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/shawnee.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Shawnee, KS

Bankruptcy in Shawnee, KS

Shawnee is a suburb in northwestern Johnson County with a population of about 67,300. It borders Kansas City, KS and Merriam, offering a mix of established neighborhoods and newer developments.

Key Filing Information

County

Johnson County

Population: 67,311 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City, KS Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66203, 66216, 66217, 66218, 66226, 66286

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Shawnee

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUNLIMITED (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit on necessary items

Personal Property$20,000 total (single) / $40,000 (married)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions.

The Kansas Advantage

Shawnee residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption. Across the state line in Missouri, homeowners can protect only $15,000 in home equity. This is one of the most dramatic exemption differences in the country.

Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married). Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Shawnee

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower than Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - higher dismissal rate across the state line

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Shawnee City Hall - 11110 Johnson Dr, Shawnee, KS 66203

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave #200, Topeka, KS 66603 (statewide intake)

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Shawnee, KS cases?

Shawnee is in Johnson County, which files in the District of Kansas. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. The D. Kan. has a 31.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate.

What exemptions apply in Shawnee, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply: unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban / 160 acres rural), $20,000 vehicle (single) or $40,000 (married), fully exempt retirement accounts. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does the Kansas side compare to Missouri for bankruptcy?

Shawnee residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption versus Missouri's $15,000, and Kansas's $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. The D. Kan. also has a lower Chapter 13 dismissal rate (31.4% vs 40.4%). Full comparison.

Can I use Missouri exemptions if I recently moved from Missouri?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Kansas for at least 730 days (2 years) before filing, you may need to use the exemptions from your prior state. If you moved from Missouri to Kansas recently, you may still be subject to Missouri's lower exemptions. Consult an attorney about your specific timeline.

What about Shawnee specifically?

Shawnee sits at the boundary between Johnson and Wyandotte counties. Both file in the District of Kansas with the same Kansas exemptions. The city's diverse housing stock means many residents can fully protect their home equity under the unlimited homestead exemption.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Smithville, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/smithville.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Smithville, MO

Bankruptcy in Smithville, MO

Smithville is a growing community in Clay County, located north of Kansas City near Smithville Lake. The city has seen steady residential growth as families seek affordable housing within commuting distance of the KC metro, with a population of approximately 10,500.

Key Filing Information

County

Clay County

Population: 10,500 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64089

Files in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Smithville

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Smithville, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different bankruptcy outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Smithville

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Clay County Northern Courthouse - 4 N Main St, Smithville, MO 64089

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Missouri 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Smithville, MO cases?

Smithville is in Clay County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Smithville, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, wildcard of $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide.

How does Smithville compare to Kansas suburbs?

Smithville residents are subject to Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption versus Missouri's $3,000. Full comparison.

What about Smithville specifically?

Smithville's growth near Smithville Lake has pushed home values up, but Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption remains fixed. Residents with homes purchased in the last decade may have equity exceeding the exemption amount.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Spring Hill, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/spring-hill.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Spring Hill, KS

Bankruptcy in Spring Hill, KS

Spring Hill is a fast-growing city in southern Johnson County, Kansas, along US-169. Once a small rural town, it has experienced explosive suburban growth with new housing developments attracting families from the KC metro.

Key Filing Information

County

Johnson County

Population: 7,600 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66083

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Spring Hill

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit (reasonable necessity)

WildcardNone (not needed with unlimited homestead)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions. Kansas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Spring Hill, KS can protect unlimited home equity and up to $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. Someone across the state line in Missouri can protect only $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Same metro area, dramatically different bankruptcy outcomes.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Spring Hill

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - lower than the Missouri side

Compare: Missouri Side

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - significantly higher dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Johnson County Courthouse - 100 N Kansas Ave, Olathe, KS 66061

Kansas Legal Services - 712 S Kansas Ave, Topeka, KS 66603 (serves statewide)

Kansas 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Spring Hill, KS cases?

Spring Hill is in Johnson County, which files in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What exemptions apply in Spring Hill, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply. Unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban), $20,000 vehicle ($40,000 married), fully exempt retirement. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Spring Hill compare to Missouri suburbs?

Kansas residents benefit from an unlimited homestead exemption, while Missouri suburbs are limited to $15,000. Kansas vehicle exemption is $20,000/$40,000 versus Missouri s $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Missouri instead of Kansas?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Missouri, you must reside there at least 730 days before Missouri exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Spring Hill specifically?

Spring Hill is one of the fastest-growing suburbs in Kansas. Residents benefit from Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption, meaning no matter how much equity you have in your home, it is protected in bankruptcy.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Sugar Creek, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/sugar-creek.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Sugar Creek, MO

Bankruptcy in Sugar Creek, MO

Sugar Creek is a small city in Jackson County, nestled between Independence and Kansas City along the Missouri River. Once an industrial community centered around a Sinclair/AMOCO refinery, it has transitioned toward residential with affordable housing.

Key Filing Information

County

Jackson County

Population: 3,300 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64054

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Sugar Creek

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Sugar Creek, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas: $20,000 / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Sugar Creek

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Sugar Creek, MO cases?

Sugar Creek is in Jackson County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Sugar Creek, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Sugar Creek compare to Kansas suburbs?

Sugar Creek residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Sugar Creek specifically?

Sugar Creek has very affordable housing, with most homes valued below the metro average. This means most homeowners' equity falls within Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption, making Chapter 7 straightforward for many residents.

## Bankruptcy in Tonganoxie, KS - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/tonganoxie.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Tonganoxie, KS

Bankruptcy in Tonganoxie, KS

Tonganoxie is a small city in Leavenworth County, Kansas, along US-24/40 between Kansas City and Lawrence. It has seen steady growth as a suburban-rural community with affordable housing relative to closer-in Johnson County suburbs.

Key Filing Information

County

Leavenworth County

Population: 5,900 (approx.)

Federal District

District of Kansas

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

66086

All file in D. Kan.

Kansas Exemptions in Tonganoxie

PropertyKansas Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Vehicle$20,000 (single) / $40,000 (married)

Household GoodsNo specific dollar limit (reasonable necessity)

WildcardNone (not needed with unlimited homestead)

Wages75% exempt

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Kansas does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Kansas state exemptions. Kansas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Tonganoxie, KS can protect unlimited home equity and up to $20,000-$40,000 in vehicle equity. Someone across the state line in Missouri can protect only $15,000 in home equity and $3,000 in vehicle equity. Same metro area, dramatically different bankruptcy outcomes.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Tonganoxie

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Tonganoxie, KS cases?

Tonganoxie is in Leavenworth County, which files in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What exemptions apply in Tonganoxie, KS?

Kansas exemptions apply. Unlimited homestead (up to 1 acre urban), $20,000 vehicle ($40,000 married), fully exempt retirement. Kansas does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Tonganoxie compare to Missouri suburbs?

Kansas residents benefit from an unlimited homestead exemption, while Missouri suburbs are limited to $15,000. Kansas vehicle exemption is $20,000/$40,000 versus Missouri s $3,000. Full comparison.

Can I file in Missouri instead of Kansas?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Missouri, you must reside there at least 730 days before Missouri exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Tonganoxie specifically?

Tonganoxie has affordable housing and Kansas's unlimited homestead exemption. This is a strong combination for bankruptcy filers - your entire home equity is protected regardless of amount.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Warrensburg, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/warrensburg.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Warrensburg, MO

Bankruptcy in Warrensburg, MO

Warrensburg is the county seat of Johnson County, Missouri (not to be confused with Johnson County, Kansas), located about 50 miles southeast of Kansas City. Home to the University of Central Missouri and near Whiteman Air Force Base, it has a student and military-influenced population.

Key Filing Information

County

Johnson County, MO

Population: 20,800 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64093

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Warrensburg

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Warrensburg, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas: $20,000 / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Warrensburg

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

District Statistics

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

40.4%

W.D. Mo. (2008-2024) - roughly 4 in 10 Ch. 13 cases dismissed

Compare: Kansas Side

31.4%

D. Kan. (2008-2024) - significantly lower dismissal rate

View full district statistics →

Local Resources

Johnson County, MO Courthouse - 300 N Holden St, Warrensburg, MO 64093

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - 1125 Grand Blvd #1900, Kansas City, MO 64106

Missouri 211 (dial 2-1-1) - Free referrals to local services including financial counseling

Full legal aid and resource directory →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Warrensburg, MO cases?

Warrensburg is in Johnson County, MO, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Warrensburg, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Warrensburg compare to Kansas suburbs?

Warrensburg residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Warrensburg specifically?

Warrensburg is a college and military town. Student loan debt (generally not dischargeable) and military-specific protections (SCRA) are common considerations. Missouri's low exemptions apply here just as they do in the KC metro.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for advice about your specific circumstances. This site does not recommend or endorse any specific attorney or law firm.

## Bankruptcy in Weatherby Lake, MO - Filing Guide, Exemptions &amp; Resources - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cities/weatherby-lake.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Weatherby Lake, MO

Bankruptcy in Weatherby Lake, MO

Weatherby Lake is a small, affluent residential community in Platte County built around a private 100-acre lake. It is one of the most exclusive neighborhoods in the Kansas City metro, with lakefront homes commanding premium prices.

Key Filing Information

County

Platte County

Population: 2,000 (approx.)

Federal District

Western District of Missouri

Kansas City Division

Bankruptcy Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and hearings held here

Zip Codes

64152

All file in W.D. Mo.

Missouri Exemptions in Weatherby Lake

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household Goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + up to $3,250 unused homestead

Wages75% exempt (90% head of household)

Retirement AccountsFully exempt

Missouri does not allow federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Missouri state exemptions.

The State Line Difference

Someone in Weatherby Lake, MO can protect $15,000 in home equity. Someone across the state line in Kansas can protect unlimited home equity. Same metro area. Vastly different outcomes.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Kansas: $20,000 / $40,000 (married).

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

See the complete exemptions guide →

Filing Bankruptcy in Weatherby Lake

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri. The automatic stay stops collections, garnishments, and foreclosures immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Cost Estimates

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (installment payments available)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in the KC area

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Weatherby Lake, MO cases?

Weatherby Lake is in Platte County, which files in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What exemptions apply in Weatherby Lake, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply. $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, $600 wildcard plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. Full guide.

How does Weatherby Lake compare to Kansas suburbs?

Weatherby Lake residents face Missouri s $15,000 homestead exemption. Kansas suburbs have unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Can I file in Kansas instead of Missouri?

No. You must file where you live. If you move to Kansas, you must reside there at least 730 days before Kansas exemptions apply under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3).

What about Weatherby Lake specifically?

Weatherby Lake is one of KC's most exclusive communities with high home values. Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption is woefully inadequate for most Weatherby Lake homeowners. Chapter 13 is almost always necessary to protect the home.

## Converting Between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 in Kansas City - When and How to Convert
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFAQOBP

Home›Converting Chapters

Converting Between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 in Kansas City

Filed the wrong chapter? Your circumstances changed? Bankruptcy law allows you to convert between chapters - sometimes as a matter of right. Here is how it works in the Western District of Missouri and the District of Kansas.

Quick answer: You can convert Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 (section 706) or Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 (section 1307) at any time, as long as the case was not previously converted from another chapter. The court fee is $25. You must be eligible for the new chapter.

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 Conversion

When It Makes Sense

Protect non-exempt assets: If the trustee identifies property you want to keep - such as home equity above Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption - converting to Chapter 13 lets you pay the value through a plan instead of surrendering the property

Save your home from foreclosure: Chapter 7 does not help you catch up on missed mortgage payments; Chapter 13 does

Save your car: If your car equity exceeds Missouri's $3,000 vehicle exemption (or Kansas's $20,000), converting lets you pay the value over time

Income increased: If your income rose after filing Chapter 7, conversion to Chapter 13 may be better than dismissal

Requirements

Case must not have been previously converted from Chapter 13 or another chapter

You must be eligible for Chapter 13: regular income and debts within limits ($2.75 million combined)

Conversion fee: $25

You will need to file a Chapter 13 plan and updated schedules

Under section 706(a), the debtor's right to convert is generally absolute if the case was not previously converted.

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 Conversion

When It Makes Sense

Lost your job: Without regular income, you cannot make Chapter 13 plan payments. Converting to Chapter 7 eliminates the debt without a payment plan.

Income decreased significantly: If your financial situation worsened, Chapter 7 may be the better path

Facing dismissal: The Western District of Missouri has a Chapter 13 dismissal rate of 40.4%. Converting to Chapter 7 before dismissal preserves your discharge rights.

No longer need to protect property: If you originally filed Chapter 13 to save a home but decided to surrender it, Chapter 7 offers faster relief

Plan payments are unsustainable: If you have fallen behind and see no way to catch up

Requirements

Case must not have been previously converted from Chapter 7

You must be eligible for Chapter 7 (may need to pass the means test)

Conversion fee: $25

You may need to file updated schedules and the means test form

No prior Chapter 7 discharge within 8 years (section 727(a)(8))

Under section 1307(a), the debtor's right to convert is generally absolute. However, the court can deny conversion in cases of bad faith.

Conversion Fees and Costs

ConversionCourt FeeTypical Attorney Fee (KC)Statutory Basis

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13$25$1,000-$2,00011 U.S.C. 706(a)

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7$25$500-$1,50011 U.S.C. 1307(a)

Chapter 7 to Chapter 11$922Varies widely11 U.S.C. 706(a)

Chapter 13 to Chapter 11$932Varies widely11 U.S.C. 1307(d)

In Chapter 13, additional attorney fees for conversion can sometimes be paid through the plan.

Key Differences: Conversion vs. Dismissal and Refiling

Conversion

Same case continues with a new case number suffix

Filing date stays the same

Automatic stay remains in effect continuously

No new filing fee (just $25 conversion fee)

Property of the estate is determined as of original filing date

Dismiss and Refile

Old case ends, new case begins

New filing date

Gap in automatic stay protection

Full new filing fee ($338 or $313)

If dismissed within 180 days, refiling triggers 30-day stay limit under section 362(c)(3)

Conversion is almost always better than dismissal and refiling because it preserves continuous automatic stay protection and avoids the section 362(c)(3) penalty.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my Chapter 7 to Chapter 13?

Yes. Under section 706(a), you have the right to convert as long as the case was not previously converted from another chapter. You must be eligible for Chapter 13 (regular income, debts within limits). The fee is $25.

Can I convert my Chapter 13 to Chapter 7?

Yes. Under section 1307(a), you can convert at any time. You must be eligible for Chapter 7. The fee is $25. This is often better than letting your Chapter 13 be dismissed.

How much does conversion cost?

The court fee is $25. Attorney fees for the additional work typically range from $500-$2,000 in Kansas City depending on the direction of conversion and complexity.

When should I convert Chapter 7 to Chapter 13?

When you need to protect non-exempt assets (home equity above $15,000 in MO, car equity above $3,000 in MO), save your home from foreclosure, or when the trustee wants to liquidate property you want to keep.

When should I convert Chapter 13 to Chapter 7?

When you lost your job, your income dropped, you are facing dismissal for missed plan payments, or you no longer need to protect property through the plan.

Do I need court approval to convert?

Generally no. The debtor's right to convert is absolute under sections 706(a) and 1307(a) as long as the case was not previously converted. The court may deny conversion only in limited circumstances such as bad faith.

Related Topics

Chapter 13

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kansas City? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/cost.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kansas City?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1K-$4K

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000 - $2,000Typical Kansas City range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Total estimate$1,400 - $2,400

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. For 2026, 150% of poverty for a single person is approximately $22,590. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500 - $4,000Typical KC range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$2,900 - $4,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

A major advantage of Chapter 13 is that attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years. This makes Chapter 13 more accessible when you cannot afford to pay everything before filing.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (only 3.8% of WDMO filers and 1.6% of D. Kansas filers do this)

Reduced-fee credit counseling - many approved agencies offer reduced fees for low-income filers

Important Cost Considerations

Compare the cost to your debt. If you owe $20,000+ in credit card debt, spending $1,500-$4,000 to eliminate it may be the best financial decision you make

Ask about flat fees. Most bankruptcy attorneys charge flat fees, not hourly rates

Beware of hidden costs. Ask if the quoted fee includes everything - filing fee, credit counseling, amendments, and the 341 meeting

Chapter 13 dismissal costs money. If your Chapter 13 is dismissed, you spent months making payments without getting a discharge. Quality representation is worth paying for

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Kansas City?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,000. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. The total is typically $1,400 to $2,400. Fee waivers are available for low-income filers. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Kansas City?

The filing fee is $313 and attorney fees typically range from $2,500 to $4,000. The key advantage is that most costs can be paid through your repayment plan, so you often need very little money upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal Aid of Western Missouri may provide free representation. Credit counseling agencies sometimes offer reduced fees for low-income filers.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment with the rest paid through monthly plan payments.

## Bankruptcy by County - Kansas City Metro Area
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Counties

Bankruptcy by County - Kansas City Metro Area

Your county determines which federal court handles your bankruptcy case and which state exemptions protect your property. Find your county below for specific information.

5 Counties in the Kansas City Metro

Jackson County, MO

Jackson County is the Missouri-side heart of Kansas City. It is the most populous county in the KC metro on the Missouri side and home to downtown Kansas City.

District: Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

Courthouse: 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Jackson County, MO bankruptcy guide →

Clay County, MO

Clay County is directly north of the Missouri River in the Kansas City metro, including Liberty, Gladstone, and North Kansas City. Cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri.

District: Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

Courthouse: 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Clay County, MO bankruptcy guide →

Platte County, MO

Platte County is northwest of Kansas City and home to KCI Airport and Parkville. Despite being less urban than Jackson County, it is part of the same federal court division.

District: Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

Courthouse: 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Platte County, MO bankruptcy guide →

Johnson County, KS

Johnson County is the most affluent and populous county on the Kansas side of the KC metro, including Overland Park, Olathe, Lenexa, and Shawnee. It offers dramatically better bankruptcy exemptions than the Missouri side.

District: District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

Courthouse: 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

Johnson County, KS bankruptcy guide →

Wyandotte County, KS

Wyandotte County is home to Kansas City, Kansas (KCK) and the Unified Government. The bankruptcy courthouse is actually located within Wyandotte County, making it the most convenient filing location in the KC metro.

District: District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

Courthouse: 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

Wyandotte County, KS bankruptcy guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which counties are in the Kansas City bankruptcy metro area?

The Kansas City metro area covers 5 counties: Jackson County, MO, Clay County, MO, Platte County, MO, Johnson County, KS, Wyandotte County, KS. Each county falls under a specific federal bankruptcy court district.

Does it matter which county I live in for bankruptcy?

Yes. Your county determines which federal district court handles your case, and potentially which state exemptions apply. Different districts may have different local rules, trustees, and dismissal rates.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Cass County, MO - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/cass-county.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Cass County, MO

Bankruptcy in Cass County, MO

Cass County is south of Jackson County in the Kansas City metro, including Belton, Raymore, Harrisonville (county seat), and Pleasant Hill. It has a mix of suburban growth in the north and rural character in the south.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Cass County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

107,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

64011, 64012, 64078, 64080, 64083, 64701

All file in Western District of Missouri

Cities in Cass County

Major cities and towns: Belton, Raymore, Harrisonville (county seat), Pleasant Hill, Peculiar, Garden City

BeltonRaymoreHarrisonvillePleasant HillAll KC Cities →

Missouri Exemptions in Cass County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Cass County (MO) has a $15,000 homestead exemption. Across the state line in Johnson County (KS), the homestead exemption is unlimited. The W.D. Mo. Chapter 13 dismissal rate is 40.4% versus 31.4% in D. Kan.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Cass County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Cass County residents file bankruptcy?

Cass County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division, at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What are the exemptions for Cass County, MO?

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Cass County compare to Kansas counties?

Cass County (MO) has a $15,000 homestead exemption. Across the state line in Johnson County (KS), the homestead exemption is unlimited. The W.D. Mo. Chapter 13 dismissal rate is 40.4% versus 31.4% in D. Kan. Full comparison.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for your specific circumstances.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Clay County, MO - Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/clay-county-mo.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Clay County, MO

Bankruptcy in Clay County, MO

Clay County is directly north of the Missouri River in the Kansas City metro, including Liberty, Gladstone, and North Kansas City. Cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Clay County, MO are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri/Kansas Exemptions in Clay County, MO

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Clay County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Clay County residents file bankruptcy?

Clay County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division, at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. This is the same courthouse used for Jackson County cases on the Missouri side.

What are the exemptions for Clay County, MO?

Missouri exemptions apply: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods. The wildcard exemption provides $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. All retirement accounts are fully exempt. These are the same exemptions for all Missouri counties.

How does Clay County compare to Johnson County, KS for bankruptcy?

Missouri (Clay County) has a $15,000 homestead exemption and $3,000 vehicle exemption. Kansas (Johnson County) has an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000 vehicle exemption. The Western District of Missouri also has a higher Chapter 13 dismissal rate than the District of Kansas.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Kansas City Area Counties

Jackson County, MO
Platte County, MO
Johnson County, KS
Wyandotte County, KS
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in Douglas County, KS - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/douglas-county.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Douglas County, KS

Bankruptcy in Douglas County, KS

Douglas County is home to Lawrence and the University of Kansas. Located about 40 miles west of Kansas City along I-70 and K-10, it has a college-town economy with a mix of student, academic, and commuter populations.

Key Information

Federal District

District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Douglas County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

120,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

66044, 66045, 66046, 66047, 66025, 66006

All file in District of Kansas

Cities in Douglas County

Major cities and towns: Lawrence (county seat), Eudora, Baldwin City, Lecompton

EudoraBaldwin CityAll KC Cities →

Kansas Exemptions in Douglas County

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Douglas County (KS) has an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000 vehicle exemption. The closest Missouri county (Jackson) has only $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle. KU students should note student loans are generally not dischargeable.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Douglas County, KS

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Douglas County residents file bankruptcy?

Douglas County cases are filed in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division, at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What are the exemptions for Douglas County, KS?

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Douglas County compare to Missouri counties?

Douglas County (KS) has an unlimited homestead exemption and $20,000 vehicle exemption. The closest Missouri county (Jackson) has only $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle. KU students should note student loans are generally not dischargeable. Full comparison.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for your specific circumstances.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Jackson County, MO - Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/jackson-county-mo.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Jackson County, MO

Bankruptcy in Jackson County, MO

Jackson County is the Missouri-side heart of Kansas City. It is the most populous county in the KC metro on the Missouri side and home to downtown Kansas City.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Jackson County, MO are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri/Kansas Exemptions in Jackson County, MO

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Jackson County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Jackson County, MO cases?

Jackson County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. This court has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate - significantly higher than the Kansas side.

What is the homestead exemption in Jackson County, MO?

Missouri's homestead exemption is only $15,000 - one of the lowest in the nation. If your home equity exceeds $15,000, Chapter 13 may be the safer option to protect your home. The wildcard exemption ($600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead) provides modest additional protection.

Should I file on the Missouri or Kansas side?

You must file where you live. Jackson County residents must file in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose to file in Kansas to get better exemptions. If you are considering moving, you must live in the new state for at least 730 days before its exemptions apply.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Kansas City Area Counties

Clay County, MO
Platte County, MO
Johnson County, KS
Wyandotte County, KS
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in Johnson County, KS - Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/johnson-county-ks.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Johnson County, KS

Bankruptcy in Johnson County, KS

Johnson County is the most affluent and populous county on the Kansas side of the KC metro, including Overland Park, Olathe, Lenexa, and Shawnee. It offers dramatically better bankruptcy exemptions than the Missouri side.

Key Information

Federal District

District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Johnson County, KS are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri/Kansas Exemptions in Johnson County, KS

Kansas state exemptions apply (no federal option). Homestead: unlimited in value, up to 1 acre in city or 160 acres on a farm. Vehicle: $20,000. Personal property: no aggregate limit but specific categories capped. Burial plot: unlimited. Head-of-household wages: 75% exempt. Retirement: fully exempt.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Johnson County, KS

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the District of Kansas (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Johnson County, KS residents file bankruptcy?

Johnson County cases are filed in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division. The courthouse is at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101. This is a different federal court than the Missouri side.

What is the homestead exemption in Johnson County, KS?

Kansas has an unlimited homestead exemption in value for properties up to 1 acre in a city or 160 acres on a farm. This is dramatically better than Missouri's $15,000 limit. Your home equity is fully protected regardless of amount.

How does Johnson County compare to Jackson County for bankruptcy?

Johnson County (KS) offers far superior exemptions: unlimited homestead vs $15,000, $20,000 vehicle vs $3,000, and a lower Chapter 13 dismissal rate (31.4% vs 40.4%). If you live in Johnson County, these Kansas exemptions automatically apply to your case.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Kansas City Area Counties

Jackson County, MO
Clay County, MO
Platte County, MO
Wyandotte County, KS
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in Lafayette County, MO - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/lafayette-county.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Lafayette County, MO

Bankruptcy in Lafayette County, MO

Lafayette County is east of Jackson County along I-70, including Lexington (county seat), Odessa, and Higginsville. It is a historically significant rural county with Civil War battlefields and affordable housing.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Lafayette County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

32,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

64067, 64076, 64037, 64096, 64020

All file in Western District of Missouri

Cities in Lafayette County

Major cities and towns: Lexington (county seat), Odessa, Higginsville, Waverly, Concordia

OdessaAll KC Cities →

Missouri Exemptions in Lafayette County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Lafayette County uses Missouri exemptions: $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle. The affordable housing market means most residents' home equity falls within the exemption. Kansas residents have an unlimited homestead exemption.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Lafayette County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Lafayette County residents file bankruptcy?

Lafayette County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division, at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What are the exemptions for Lafayette County, MO?

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Lafayette County compare to Kansas counties?

Lafayette County uses Missouri exemptions: $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle. The affordable housing market means most residents' home equity falls within the exemption. Kansas residents have an unlimited homestead exemption. Full comparison.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Missouri for your specific circumstances.

## Bankruptcy in Leavenworth County, KS - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/leavenworth-county.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Leavenworth County, KS

Bankruptcy in Leavenworth County, KS

Leavenworth County is northwest of Wyandotte County, including Leavenworth (county seat), Lansing, Basehor, and Tonganoxie. It is home to Fort Leavenworth, the Lansing Correctional Facility, and the University of Saint Mary.

Key Information

Federal District

District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Leavenworth County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

82,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

66048, 66043, 66007, 66086, 66020

All file in District of Kansas

Cities in Leavenworth County

Major cities and towns: Leavenworth (county seat), Lansing, Basehor, Tonganoxie, Easton

LansingBasehorTonganoxieAll KC Cities →

Kansas Exemptions in Leavenworth County

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Leavenworth County (KS) has an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000 vehicle exemption ($40,000 married). Missouri counties across the metro have only a $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle exemption.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Leavenworth County, KS

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Leavenworth County residents file bankruptcy?

Leavenworth County cases are filed in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division, at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What are the exemptions for Leavenworth County, KS?

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Leavenworth County compare to Missouri counties?

Leavenworth County (KS) has an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000 vehicle exemption ($40,000 married). Missouri counties across the metro have only a $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle exemption. Full comparison.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for your specific circumstances.

## Bankruptcy in Miami County, KS - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/miami-county-ks.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Miami County, KS

Bankruptcy in Miami County, KS

Miami County is south of Johnson County in the Kansas City metro, including Paola (county seat), Louisburg, and Osawatomie. It has a rural-suburban character with growing bedroom communities in the northern portion.

Key Information

Federal District

District of Kansas (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Miami County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

34,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

66071, 66053, 66064, 66083

All file in District of Kansas

Cities in Miami County

Major cities and towns: Paola (county seat), Louisburg, Osawatomie, Spring Hill (partial)

PaolaSpring HillAll KC Cities →

Kansas Exemptions in Miami County

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Miami County (KS) has the same Kansas exemptions as Johnson County: unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle. Across the state line in Cass County (MO), you get only $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Miami County, KS

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the District of Kansas (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Miami County residents file bankruptcy?

Miami County cases are filed in the District of Kansas, Kansas City Division, at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101.

What are the exemptions for Miami County, KS?

Kansas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited (up to 1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). Vehicle: $20,000 ($40,000 married). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Miami County compare to Missouri counties?

Miami County (KS) has the same Kansas exemptions as Johnson County: unlimited homestead and $20,000/$40,000 vehicle. Across the state line in Cass County (MO), you get only $15,000 homestead and $3,000 vehicle. Full comparison.

Disclaimer: This page provides general educational information about bankruptcy, not legal advice. Consult a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Kansas for your specific circumstances.

## Bankruptcy in Platte County, MO - Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/platte-county-mo.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Platte County, MO

Bankruptcy in Platte County, MO

Platte County is northwest of Kansas City and home to KCI Airport and Parkville. Despite being less urban than Jackson County, it is part of the same federal court division.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Platte County, MO are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri/Kansas Exemptions in Platte County, MO

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement accounts: fully exempt. No federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Platte County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which court handles Platte County, MO bankruptcies?

Platte County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division, at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106. Platte County residents are typically 20-30 minutes from the courthouse.

Are Platte County exemptions the same as Jackson County?

Yes. All Missouri counties use the same state exemptions: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle, $3,000 household goods, and the wildcard. There are no county-specific exemptions in Missouri.

Can I protect my home near KCI Airport in bankruptcy?

Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption is quite limited. If your home equity exceeds $15,000, consider Chapter 13, which allows you to keep your home while repaying debts over 3-5 years. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Wyandotte County, KS

## Bankruptcy in Ray County, MO - Filing Guide &amp; Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcy.org/counties/ray-county.html

Kansas City Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Ray County, MO

Bankruptcy in Ray County, MO

Ray County is a rural county northeast of Kansas City, including Richmond (county seat) and portions of Excelsior Springs. It has a largely agricultural economy with a population spread across small towns.

Key Information

Federal District

Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Ray County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

341 meetings and court hearings held here.

County Population

23,000

Approximate population

Major Zip Codes

64084, 64085, 64024, 64063, 64071

All file in Western District of Missouri

Cities in Ray County

Major cities and towns: Richmond (county seat), Excelsior Springs (partial), Lawson, Orrick, Hardin

Excelsior SpringsAll KC Cities →

Missouri Exemptions in Ray County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

MO vs KS Comparison

Ray County uses the same Missouri exemptions as all other Missouri counties: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle. Kansas counties across the metro have an unlimited homestead exemption.

Full Missouri vs. Kansas comparison →

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Ray County, MO

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Western District of Missouri (Kansas City Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: ~60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Ray County residents file bankruptcy?

Ray County cases are filed in the Western District of Missouri, Kansas City Division, at 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

What are the exemptions for Ray County, MO?

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement: fully exempt.

How does Ray County compare to Kansas counties?

Ray County uses the same Missouri exemptions as all other Missouri counties: $15,000 homestead, $3,000 vehicle. Kansas counties across the metro have an unlimited homestead exemption. Full comparison.
